title-group.article-title: RPE65 is present in human green/red cones and promotes photopigment regeneration in an in vitro cone cell model

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Neuroscience, Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, SC 29425
2Department of Physiology, University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center, Oklahoma City, OK 73104
3Department of Ophthalmology, Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, SC 29425

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding author: Storm Eye Institute, Medical University of South Carolina, 167 Ashley Avenue, Charleston, SC 29425; phone: (843) 792-2814; fax: (843) 792-1723; crouchrk@musc.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

abstract.p: RPE65 is an abundantly expressed protein within the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) of the eye that is required for retinoid metabolism to support vision. Its genetic mutations are linked to the congenital disease Leber congenital amaurosis Type 2 (LCA2) characterized by the early onset of central vision loss. Current gene therapy trials have targeted restoration of functional RPE65 within the RPE of these patients with some success. Recent data show RPE65 is also present within mouse cones to promote function. In this study, we evaluated the presence of RPE65 in human cones and investigated its potential mechanism for supporting cone function in the 661W cone cell line. We found that RPE65 was selectively expressed in human green/red cones but absent from blue cones and mediated ester hydrolysis for photopigment synthesis in vitro. These data suggest that cone RPE65 supports human diurnal vision, potentially enhancing our strategies for treating LCA2.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Visual function is dependent upon light detection by rod and cone photoreceptors through photopigments formed from the chromophore 11-cis retinyl-aldehyde (RAL) bound to opsin G-protein-coupled receptors. Human cones mediate daytime color vision through one of three forms of opsins to detect blue (short-wavelength; S-), green (mid-wavelength; M-), or red (long-wavelength; L-) light. When light bleaches the photopigment, 11-cis RAL isomerizes to the all-trans conformation, triggering the phototransduction cascade. Finally, all-trans RAL disassociates from the opsin and is reduced to all-trans retinyl-alcohol (ROL) by retinol dehydrogenases (RDHs). To regenerate photopigment, all-trans ROL is converted back to 11-cis RAL through an enzymatic process called the visual cycle (Travis et al., 2007) (Figure 1).

RPE65 protein plays a critical role in 11-cis RAL synthesis within the eye. It is essential for all-trans to 11-cis retinoid isomerization (Jin et al., 2005; Moiseyev et al., 2005; Redmond et al., 2005), established through Rpe65−/− mouse studies where 11-cis RAL synthesis was blocked and the substrate, all-trans retinyl-ester (RE), accumulated (Redmond et al., 1998). Furthermore, the lack of endogenous chromophore production caused early cone degeneration (Znoiko et al., 2005). Human RPE65 mutations underlie the congenital disease LCA2, characterized by severe loss of vision, sensory nystagmus, amaurotic pupils, and absent signals on electroretinography (ERG) (den-Hollander et al., 2008). Since the RPE is classically accepted as the exclusive region within the eye where RPE65 metabolizes retinoids, gene therapy trials have targeted this area with moderate rescue of visual function in LCA2 patients (Cideciyan, 2010).

An alternate visual cycle may exist within the vertebrate retina to support cone photopigment regeneration independent of the RPE (Wang and Kefalov, 2011) (Figure 1). The unidentified isomerase is believed to localize within Müller glia (Das et al., 1992; Muniz et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2009; Wang and Kefalov, 2009), and 11-cis ROL production may underlie its cone specificity since cones can oxidize 11-cis ROL but rods cannot (Jones et al., 1989; Parker et al., 2011). Complete regeneration of cone photopigments necessitates both alternate and RPE visual cycles, where the former is fast and promotes initial rapid dark adaptation and the latter is slow and is required to complete the process (Kolesnikov et al., 2011).

RPE65 may play a novel role within the alternate visual cycle. Evidence first arose from the discovery of mRNA in salamander cones (Ma et al., 1998) and the protein in mammalian cones through immunohistochemistry (IHC) (Znoiko et al., 2002). We recently localized RPE65 to the outer segment (OS) region of the mouse cone and found that mouse strains with lower amounts of cone RPE65 were less efficient at regenerating cone photopigment than those with higher levels (Tang et al., 2011).

The mechanism through which cone RPE65 operates is not understood. Furthermore, its relevance to human vision has not been established. We addressed these issues in the current study through IHC analysis of cones from donor human eyes and characterization of retinoid metabolism in an in vitro cone model (661W cells) (Tan et al., 2004) that has been genetically modified to express RPE65.

sec.title: Human tissue
sec.p: Human eyes were obtained from three donors (ages 11, 21, and 85 years). The 11 and 21 year-old donor eyes were from male donors and were acquired from the National Disease Research Interchange with assistance from Zsolt Ablonczy (Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, SC). The 85 year-old donor eyes were from a female donor and were acquired from the San Diego Eye Bank with assistance from Anne Hanneken (Scripps Research Institute, La Jolla, CA).

sec.title: Anti-RPE65 specific primary antibodies
sec.p: The PETLET polyclonal rabbit antibody has been previously described (Znoiko et al., 2002) and was used at a concentration of 2 μg/ml for IHC analysis and 0.2 μg/ml for immunoblot analysis. The DALEED polyclonal rabbit antibody has been previously described (Moiseyev et al., 2008) and was used at a dilution of 2 μg/ml for IHC analysis and 0.2 μg/ml for immunoblot analysis. For the target epitope sequence of each antibody, please refer to Table 1.

sec.title: Other primary antibodies
sec.p: The following antibodies were used: anti-S and M/L-cone opsins goat polyclonal antibodies (Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc., Santa Cruz, CA), at 2 μg/ml for IHC; anti-GFP FITC-conjugated polyclonal rabbit antibody (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA), at 2 μg/ml for IHC; anti-human cone arrestin monoclonal 7G6 antibody was generously provided by Wolfgang Baehr (University of Utah, Salt Lake City, UT) and used as previously described (Zhang et al., 2003); anti-rod opsin 1D4 monoclonal antibody and anti-β-actin monoclonal mouse antibody were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO) and were used at 0.05 μg/ml and 0.5 μg/ml, respectively, for immunoblots.

sec.title: Generation of monoclonal population of stable RPE65-expressing 661W cells
sec.p: The 661W cell line was a generous gift from Muayyad R. Al-Ubaidi (University of Oklahoma) and cultured according to previously published protocol (Tan et al., 2004). The mouse Rpe65 cDNA (Accession# BC130028), encoding for the Leu450 variant, was obtained from Open Biosystems (Huntsville, AL). The Rpe65 cDNA was subcloned into pIRES-EGFP vector (Clontech, Mountain View, CA) and sequence was verified (MUSC Nucleic Acid Analysis Facility). For transfection, Lipofectamine 2000 reagent (Invitrogen) and Opti-MEM I reduced-serum medium (Invitrogen) were used per the manufacturer’s instructions. Afterwards, cells were subjected to selection pressure using 1.5 mg/ml G418 antibiotic (Mediatech, Manassas, VA). Resistant clones were isolated by flow cytometry based on GFP fluorescence (MUSC Flow Cytometry Facility). As a control, 661W cells were transfected with the pIRES-EGFP vector lacking Rpe65 cDNA. A group of the monoclonal Rpe65-transfected 661W cells were further transfected with a pcDNA3.1-hygro vector (Invitrogen) containing mouse rod opsin cDNA (Accession # BC031766), obtained from Open Biosystems. A stable cell line expressing both RPE65 and rod opsin was generated through selection pressure using 1.5 mg/ml hygromycin (Thermo Scientific, Rochester, NY). For the duration of all experiments, transfected cells were cultured in selection media.

sec.title: Immunoblot analysis
sec.p: To prepare human RPE homogenates, the cornea and lens were removed, and the retina was dissected away from the remaining eyecup. The RPE/choroid was then peeled away from the sclera. Procedures for the preparation of cell lysates have been previously described (Tan et al., 2004). Samples were homogenized in 1% sodium dodecyl sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich) using sonication on ice (3X, 10 seconds each), loaded onto 4–12% gradient gel (NuPAGE Bis-Tris; Invitrogen) and then transferred to nitrocellulose membrane; membranes were probed with the appropriate primary and secondary antibodies, then stripped and reprobed for β-actin. Bands were visualized with ECL imaging equipment (VersaDoc; Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA). The peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies used were goat anti-rabbit IgG and horse anti-mouse IgG (1 ng/ml concentration; Vector, Burlingame, CA).

sec.title: Immunohistochemistry
sec.p: To prepare human retina flatmounts, the retina was dissected away from the eyecup. The retina was then fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) in 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 for 1.25 hours, at room temperature (RT), then washed with PBS and blocked for 1 hour at RT with a solution containing 1% bovine serum albumin (Sigma-Aldrich), 1% rabbit serum (Sigma-Aldrich), and 0.25% Triton X-100 (Sigma-Aldrich) in PBS. Retinas were then incubated overnight at 4°C with primary antibodies diluted in a solution containing 1% Tween20 (Bio-Rad) and 1% Triton X-100 in PBS, washed and then incubated with the appropriate secondary antibodies diluted in PBS for 2 hours at RT. Retinas were cover-slip mounted with Fluoromount-G (Southern Biotech, Birmingham, AL) for analysis by fluorescence microscopy (Axioplan II; Zeiss, Jena, Germany) with appropriate filters.

To prepare frozen human eyecup sections, the cornea and lens were removed, the remaining eyecup was bisected and fixed as described above. Eyecups were washed in PBS and incubated in 30% sucrose in PBS overnight at 4°C. Eyecups were embedded in OCT (Ted Pella, Inc., Redding, CA), flash frozen in 2-methylbutane (Sigma-Aldrich) on dry ice, and cut with a cryo-microtome to produce 10 μm sections; sections were processed for staining as described above. To visualize the nuclei and inner segments, sections were incubated with propidium iodide (0.5 μg/ml; Sigma-Aldrich) diluted in PBS for 2 hours at RT. Images were acquired by confocal microscopy (Leica TCS SP5 AOBS Confocal Microscope System; Leica Microsystems, Exton, PA).

To prepare 661W cells for staining, cells were grown on chamber slides (Thermo Scientific) and fixed in freshly prepared 4% PFA for 3 minutes. Slides were washed in PBS and processed for staining as described for retina flatmounts.

sec.title: Retinoid delivery
sec.p: All retinoids were handled under dim red light. 11-cis ROL was synthesized within our laboratory. All-trans ROL was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 11-cis or all-trans ROL was suspended in ethanol and mixed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH=7.4) containing 2% BSA as a carrier. Next, solution was mixed with fetal bovine serum and then combined with cell growth media for a final retinoid concentration of 10 μM (Kanan et al., 2008), since 661W cells have been shown to tolerate this well for long-term incubation (Kanan et al., 2008), and our evaluation of cell survival after incubating across a range of concentrations (0.25–20 μM) for 24 hours in the dark confirmed these results. Following incubation, cells were washed five-times with 1 ml of ice-cold PBS, collected with a cell scraper, pelleted through centrifugation (500 × g, 3 min), and retinoids were extracted. A concentration of 10 μM of all-trans ROL was used for the incubation media since

sec.title: Retinoid extraction and HPLC analysis
sec.p: The cell pellet was resuspended in 300 μl of PBS and sonicated 2 times for 10 seconds each. Isolated retinas (n = 6) were homogenized in 200 μl of PBS using a micro-tissue grinder. Methanol (300 μl) and hydroxylamine (60 μl, 1 mol/L in PBS) were added and samples were vortexed (30 seconds). After 5 minutes at 22°C, methylene chloride (300 μl) was added, and the samples were vortexed (30 sec) and centrifuged (14,000g, 1 min). The lower phase was separated, dried under argon, and dissolved in HPLC mobile phase (11.2% ethyl acetate/2.0% dioxane/1.4% octanol in hexane, 90 ml). Separation of retinoids were performed using a Lichrosphere SI-60, 5-mm column (Alltech, Lexington, KY) and quantified by comparison of retention times and absorption properties with pure retinoid isomeric standards (Groenendijk et al., 1980). To calculate the amount of 11-cis and all-trans RE, the RE peak was collected from HPLC, dried under argon, and saponified according to a previously published protocol(Saari et al., 1993).

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Differences in retinoid levels extracted from various groups of cells were tested using two-way ANOVA and Tukey post hoc tests for individual means. A two-sided P-value < 0.05 was considered significant. All analyses were conducted using SAS version 9.1 (SAS Institute, Cary, NC).

sec.title: RPE65 is localized within the OS of human green/red cones
sec.p: To investigate if RPE65 is localized within human cones, two polyclonal antibodies raised against distinct epitopes of RPE65 were used that have been previously shown to exhibit high specificity in mouse (Redmond and Hamel, 2000; Tang et al., 2011) and chicken (Moiseyev et al., 2008) (see Table 1). We evaluated the specificities of both antibodies for human RPE65 in the RPE, as this is the region of the eye where the protein is widely accepted to be localized and to function. The RPE was obtained from donor eyes and homogenized for immunoblotting. We found that both antibodies detected a ~63 KD major band in RPE homogenates corresponding to the known size of the protein (Fig. 2A). Frozen sections of dissected RPE were used for IHC staining, and both antibodies showed robust staining within the RPE (Fig. 2B). These results confirmed the high specificity of the antibodies for human RPE65.

To determine if RPE65 immunostaining was localized in rods or cones, we co-stained human eyecup sections for RPE65 and either rhodopsin or cone arrestin, and found that RPE65 was present only in cones (Fig. 3, A–C). Results from staining with both anti-RPE65 antibodies confirmed that this is not specific to a single antibody (Fig. 2C). Since the Müller glia has been implicated to synthesize 11-cis ROL as part of the alternate visual cycle, the presence of RPE65 within this cell type was also evaluated. Co-staining for RPE65 and glutamine synthetase (GS), a glial marker, showed a lack of co-localization, indicating RPE65 was absent from the Müller glia (Fig. 3D).

We noticed a few cones stained for cone arrestin but not for RPE65 in our previous preparation, prompting further studies to determine if RPE65 was expressed uniformly among the three different subtypes of human cones. Using opsin specific antibodies for IHC, we were not able to discriminate between green and red cones since the antibody detects both M- and L-opsins; however, we were able to discriminate blue cones. Human retinas were co-stained for RPE65 and either M/L- or S-opsin, flatmounted onto a slide, and analyzed. We found that RPE65 immunostaining localized to the OS of green/red cones but was absent from blue cones (Fig. 3, E&F). IHC analysis of frozen sections confirmed these findings (Fig. 3, G&H), suggesting human blue cones do not express RPE65. Our data were collected from the peripheral human retina, as we lacked well-preserved foveal samples for analysis; however, we believe these findings are applicable to foveal cones since their normal function in bright light warrants high photopigment turnover. We conclude that RPE65 is present within human cones and that there is a selective expression of the protein among the different cone subtypes.

sec.title: 661W cells are modified to express RPE65
sec.p: To evaluate the possible mechanism by which cone RPE65 supports photopigment synthesis, we utilized an in vitro cone cell model based on the 661W cell. This cell line is of mouse origin and demonstrates numerous cellular and biochemical characteristics of cones, including the expression of both types of mouse cone opsins (M- and S-), transducin, and arrestin; however, RPE65 is absent (Tan et al., 2004; Kanan et al., 2008). A monoclonal population of stable RPE65-expressing 661W cells was generated through transfection with a plasmid construct containing cassettes encoding for mouse RPE65 and green fluorescent protein (GFP) as a reporter. For control, the plasmid devoid of Rpe65 cDNA was used. Successful expression of RPE65 in transfected 661W cells was verified through immunoblotting and IHC (Fig. 4).

sec.title: RPE65 does not mediate an isomerase activity in cones in vitro
sec.p: Previous studies have shown that 661W cells possess an unidentified mechanism for generating RE (presumably the all-trans isoform) from all-trans ROL that is independent of lecithin retinol acyltransferase (LRAT) or acyl CoA retinol acyltransferase (ARAT) (Kanan et al., 2008), the two enzymes catalyzing retinoid esterification in the RPE and alternate visual cycles, respectively. Since all-trans RE has been established as the substrate for the RPE65-mediated isomerase reaction to generate 11-cis ROL within the RPE (Moiseyev et al., 2003), our initial studies were to determine if RPE65 would mediate a similar activity with endogenously synthesized all-trans RE in 661W cells. After 24 hours of incubation in the dark with media containing 10 μM of all-trans ROL, retinoids were extracted from cells and analyzed using high-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC). Rpe65-transfected and control 661W cells appeared to synthesize similar amounts of all-trans RE (Fig. 5B). Only all-trans retinoids (RE, ROL, or RAL) were detected in these cells (Fig. 5A) or in the incubation media (Fig. 6A), but 11-cis retinoids were not observed.

A possible explanation for the lack of 11-cis retinoid synthesis in Rpe65-transfected 661W cells could be the absence of photopigment bleaching to drive the isomerase reaction. Thus, to simultaneously bleach photopigments while preserving endogenous retinoids within the cells, we used a long wavelength light (LWL; ≥ 500 nm; ~1,000 lux) to bleach M-opsin photopigments. If the RPE65-mediated isomerase activity were to be driven by this process, a decrease in the amount of all-trans RE (utilized for 11-cis RAL synthesis) and/or an increase in the amount of all-trans RAL (result of photopigment bleaching) would be expected. Cells were incubated with all-trans ROL as previously described, washed thoroughly, incubated in retinoid-free media, and exposed for 5 minutes to LWL light. We found no significant changes in the amounts of all-trans RE or all-trans RAL after Rpe65-transfected 661W cells were bleached (Fig. 5, B&C), suggesting that the isomerase function classically attributed to RPE65 in the RPE is not present in our in vitro cone cell model.

sec.title: RPE65 mediates an ester hydrolase activity in cones in vitro
sec.p: Since cones have been shown to generate chromophore from 11-cis ROL synthesized by the Müller glia as part of the alternate visual cycle (Parker et al., 2011), we incubated our cells in media containing 11-cis ROL (10 μM) under similar conditions as described above. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) served as an adequate carrier and maintained a significant proportion of 11-cis ROL in the incubation media after 24 hours; however, a minute amount of all-trans ROL was detected, presumably generated from the thermoisomerization of 11-cis ROL (Fig. 6B). Similar amounts of 11-cis and all-trans REs were detected in both Rpe65-transfected and control 661W cells (Fig. 7, B&C). Interestingly, the amount of 11-cis RE produced from incubation with 11-cis ROL was 2-fold greater than the amount of all-trans RE produced from incubation with all-trans ROL across all groups tested. These data suggest that the unidentified esterification activity within 661W cells is more efficient with 11-cis ROL as a substrate than with all-trans ROL.

While 11-cis ROL was detected in Rpe65-transfected 661W cells, possibly due to the inadequate washing away of incubation media, 11-cis RAL was absent. Again, we considered the possibility that photopigment bleaching could drive chromophore synthesis in this model system. Rpe65-transfected and control 661W cells were incubated with 11-cis ROL for 24 hours in the dark, washed thoroughly, incubated in retinoid-free media, and bleached with LWL for 5 minutes. We observed that LWL bleaching of Rpe65-transfected cells caused a decrease in 11-cis RE (~ 0.93 nmol/plate; Fig. 7B) proportional to an increase in the amount of all-trans RAL detected (~ 0.87 nmol/plate; Fig. 7F). No significant differences in the amount of 11-cis RE or all-trans RAL were observed when control 661W cells were similarly bleached (Fig. 7, B&F). These data suggest that photopigment bleaching triggers RPE65 to mobilize 11-cis RE for chromophore production, possibly through mediating the hydrolysis of the ester bond to release 11-cis ROL for oxidation.

sec.title: Oxidation of 11-cis ROL occurs within 661W cells
sec.p: While our data show that bleaching Rpe65-transfected 661W cells with LWL light drives the utilization of 11-cis RE to regenerate photopigment, we had thus far been unable to detect 11-cis RAL in retinoids extracted from these cells. This could be explained by the unstable nature of the cone photopigment, which has a much higher tendency to spontaneously dissociate into 11-cis RAL and the apo-opsin protein when compared to the more stable rod photopigment (Matsumoto et al., 1975; Kefalov et al., 2005), allowing for degradation of the chromophore to take place. Therefore, Rpe65-transfected 661W cells were secondarily transfected to express rhodopsin (RPE65/rhodopsin) (Fig. 4A) so that the rod opsin could serve as a sink for capturing and maintaining any 11-cis RAL that may be generated. We observed detectable amounts of 11-cis RAL in RPE65/rhodopsin-transfected cells after incubation with 11-cis ROL for 24 hours in the dark, but none was detected when cells were incubated with all-trans ROL (Fig. 8). These results confirm the synthesis of 11-cis RAL in 661W cells expressing RPE65 and are the first to show the existence of an 11-cis ROL oxidase activity within this mouse cone cell line. Additional support for the existence of the oxidase activity comes from the similar profiles of known photoreceptor RDHs expressed by both 661W cells and cones of the Neural retina leucine zipper knockout (Nrl−/−) mouse (Kanan et al., 2008), which have also been suggested to possess an 11-cis ROL oxidase activity (Parker et al., 2011). Additional studies are necessary to determine if this oxidase activity is mediated by a novel RDH or by a previously uncharacterized function of a known RDH.

sec.title: RE synthesis in cones
sec.p: The isomerase of the alternate visual cycle is believed to exist within the Müller glia to catalyze a direct all-trans to 11-cis ROL conversion (Das et al., 1992; Mata et al., 2002; Mata et al., 2005; Wang and Kefalov, 2011), where 11-cis ROL is subsequently shuttled to cones to produce 11-cis RAL. Our current results from the 661W cell line indicate that the existence of an intermediate esterase synthesis step is crucial for the cone to synthesis 11-cis RAL from incoming 11-cis ROL. 11-cis RE synthesis in the cone could serve a similar storage role as observed with all-trans RE synthesis in the RPE, where a readily available pool of substrate is essential for the production of chromophore to meet functional demands. Cones can utilize 11-cis ROL for photopigment formation (Jones et al., 1989; Parker et al., 2011), presumably through a novel oxidase activity. This may underlie the specificity of the alternate visual cycle for cones, as rods appear to lack the oxidase and cannot utilize 11-cis ROL for chromophore synthesis (Kono et al., 2008; Ala-Laurila et al., 2009).

sec.title: RPE65 function in cones
sec.p: The role of RPE65 in retinoid metabolism has been characterized extensively within the RPE; however, it’s role within cones has not been studied (Wang and Kefalov, 2011). Processes from the Müller glia make numerous associations with the cone cell body as(Uehara et al., 1990), and 11-cis ROL is thought to be delivered to this region of the cell (Wang et al., 2009). We hypothesize that RPE65 could be performing three roles within cones. First, 11-cis ROL may be esterified within the cone cell body and/or inner segment (IS) and RPE65-mediated ester hydrolysis is occurring at the OS interface, possibly at the connecting cilium (CC), thus explaining why the protein is localized densely in this region of the mouse cone (Tang et al., 2011). Second, RPE65 may act as a retinoid binding protein (RBP) to stabilize 11-cis ROL and to shuttle it along the OS. Evidence for this comes from the punctate appearance of RPE65 immunostaining in the apical regions of the mouse cone OS (Tang et al., 2011) and the recent discovery that the actin-based motor protein myosin VIIa (MYO7A) mediates translocation of RPE65 within the mouse RPE (Lopes et al., 2011). MYO7A may be involved in trafficking RPE65 within the cone, since MYO7A has been localized robustly at the CC region of human and rodent photoreceptors (Liu et al., 1997) and is required for the normal trafficking of opsins through the ciliary plasma membrane to the OS (Liu et al., 1999). Finally, the possible RBP role of RPE65 may enhance 11-cis ROL oxidation increasing the probability of the retinoid for interacting with RDHs (Hemati et al., 2005; Kiser et al., 2009). The current results from the in vitro cone cell model may explain why mouse strains with a higher abundance of cone RPE65 were more efficient at synthesizing cone photopigments than those with a lower abundance of the protein (Tang et al., 2011). Additional studies using subcellular imaging techniques, such as immuno-electron microscopy, and further characterization of the unidentified 11-cis ROL oxidase activity are essential for clarifying the mechanism of cone RPE65.

sec.title: RPE65 expression in human cone subtypes
sec.p: This study is the first to establish the relevance of cone RPE65 for human vision, as we have shown the protein is localized to the OS of human cones (Fig. 3). Surprisingly, RPE65 was detected in green/red cones but not in blue cones, suggesting that green/red cones have privileged access to an additional pool of retinoids for chromophore synthesis, whereas blue cones rely solely upon the RPE.

Can the absence of RPE65 expression in human blue cones be related to human diurnal vision being more dependent on green and red cones than on blue cones? A large proportion of short-wavelength light that the eye receives from the surrounding environment is filtered out by the cornea and lens before reaching the retina (Boettner and Wolter, 1962), diminishing the contribution of blue cones for human vision. Furthermore, a recent study in mice suggested that full recovery of mouse cone photopigments was biphasic, with the alternate visual cycle mediating the initial fast phase and the slower RPE visual cycle completing the process (Kolesnikov et al., 2011). Blue cones compose less than 6% of the total primate cone population (Roorda et al., 2001) and are absent from the fovea of the human retina (Curcio et al., 1991), a pure-cone region where fast cone photopigment regeneration is essential to mediate central vision in bright light. It is possible that both visual cycles (RPE and alternate) supply human green/red cones with chromophore to support rapid photopigment regeneration for central vision, but the less essential blue cone can only access the slower RPE visual cycle.

sec.title: Implications on the therapeutic strategy for LCA2
sec.p: The results from this study may compel clinicians to reconsider the tissue targets of gene therapy in the attempt to rescue and preserve diurnal vision in LCA2 patients. The current strategy focuses on restoration of functional RPE65 in the RPE, based on the widely accepted belief that the protein exists and functions exclusively within this region of the eye. Can the efficacy of this powerful therapeutic method be enhanced if cones were also targeted for restoration? Our results show that RPE65 is indeed present within human cones and offer an initial explanation for its contribution to photopigment synthesis. Further investigation of the role of RPE65 within the cone in vivo will lead to a better understanding of the pathogenesis of LCA2 and improve the therapies that we can offer to patients who suffer from this devastating eye disease.

ack.p: The authors thank Luanna Bartholomew, Jie Fan, Patrice Goletz, Peter Kalivas, Masahiro Kono, Yiannis Koutalos, Ryan Parker, Bärbel Rohrer (Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, SC), Vladimir Kefalov (Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis, MO) and Gennadiy Moiseyev (University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center, Oklahoma City, OK) for technical assistance, discussions, and manuscript revisions. This study was supported in part by grants from the National Institutes of Health R01 EY04939 (RKC), R01 EY012231 (JXM), and C06 RR015455 (MUSC); Foundation Fighting Blindness, Inc. (RKC); and an unrestricted grant (Department of Ophthalmology, MUSC) from Research to Prevent Blindness (RPB), the RPB Senior Scientific Investigator Award (RKC), and the RPB Medical Student Research Fellowship (PHT).

Downregulation of cone-specific gene expression and degeneration of cone photoreceptors in the RPE65−/− mouse at early ages

caption.title: Schematic of the retinoid visual cycle
caption.p: ROL = retinol, RAL = retinal, RE = retinyl-ester, REH = retinyl-ester hydrolase, RDH = retinol dehydrogenase

caption.title: Anti-RPE65 antibodies detect RPE65 in human tissue samples
caption.p: Immunoblot analysis using either PETLET or DALEED anti-RPE65 antibody detected a ~63 KD major band corresponding to RPE65 in homogenates prepared from human RPE (A). Both antibodies also exhibited similar patterns of RPE65 staining in the RPE (B) and OS (C) regions of frozen sections prepared from human eyecups. Scale bars (μm) are 5 for B and 15 for C. PI = propidium iodide; trans = transmitted light.

caption.title: RPE65 is detected within human green/red cone OS
caption.p: Images are acquired from sections prepared from a young donor eye (age 11). Morphologic analysis indicates that RPE65 is present in both the RPE and OS layers of the retina (A). Co-staining with cone arrestin (CAR), rhodopsin, or glutamine synthetase (gs) show that RPE65 is localized within the cone OS (B) but absent from rods (C) and the Müller glia (D). Pieces of the retina were flatmounted (E,F) or sectioned (G,H) and co-stained for RPE65 and either M/L-opsin or S-opsin, showing presence of RPE65 in green/red cones (E,G) but not in blue cones (F,H). RPE = retinal pigment epithelium; OS = outer segment; IS = inner segment; ONL = outer nuclear layer; OPL = outer plexiform layer; INL = inner nuclear layer. Scale bars (μm) are 20 for A,D; 10 for B,H; 5 for C; 7 for G, and 40 for E,F. White arrows in H indicate presence of blue cone OS. Trans = transmitted light.

caption.title: 661W cells are genetically modified to express RPE65
caption.p: Immunoblot analysis is used to confirm the expression of various proteins in different groups of 661W cells (A). Expression of RPE65 is confirmed through IHC (B). DIC = differential interference contrast. Scale bar = 2 μm.

caption.title: RPE65 does not mediate an isomerase activity within 661W cells
caption.p: Rpe65-transfected and control 661W cells were incubated with 10 μM of all-trans ROL for 24 hours in dark conditions. Cells were subsequently washed, incubated in retinoid-free media, left in the dark or bleached with LWL light for 5 minutes, and retinoids were extracted and analyzed using HPLC. Chromatographs at 320 nm (top) and 360 nm (bottom) absorbance are shown for retinoids extracted from dark-reared (blue) or LWL light-bleached (red) Rpe65-transfected 661W cells (A). No significant (P > 0.05) differences were observed in the quantities of all-trans RE (B, right), all-trans RAL (C, right), or all-trans ROL (D, right) detected between the two groups of cells reared in the dark. Furthermore, bleaching with LWL light did not produce significant (P > 0.05) changes among the three forms of retinoids detected (B,C,D; left). 11-cis retinoids (RE, ROL, or RAL) were not detected in either group of cells, suggesting the lack of an isomerase activity. n = 7 for each condition. Error bars represent s.d.

caption.title: Retinoid profiles of incubation media
caption.p: Rpe65-transfected 661W cells were incubated in media that was loaded with 10 μM of either all-trans ROL or 11-cis ROL for 24 hours. After the incubation period, retinoids were extracted from the media and analyzed by HPLC. All-trans retinoids were detected in all-trans ROL-loaded media after incubation; however, 11-cis retinoids were absent (A). BSA served as an adequate carrier and maintained a significant amount of 11-cis ROL in the incubation media after 24 hours (B).

caption.title: RPE65 may mediate an ester hydrolase activity within 661W cells
caption.p: Rpe65-transfected and control 661W cells were incubated with 10 μM of 11-cis ROL and analyzed as previously described for Figure 4. Chromatographs at 320 nm (top) and 360 nm (bottom) absorbance are shown for retinoids extracted from dark-reared (blue) or LWL light-bleached (red) Rpe65-transfected 661W cells (A). There was no significant (P > 0.05) difference observed in the quantities of 11-cis RE (B, right) or all-trans RE (C, right) between dark-reared Rpe65-transfected and control 661W cells; however, the quantity of 11-cis RE (RPE65: 2.04 ± 0.19 nmol/plate; control: 2.29 ± 0.23 nmol/plate) was observed to be approximately two-fold greater than the quantity of all-trans RE detected (RPE65: 1.39 ± 0.17 nmol/plate; control: 1.21 ± 0.13 nmol/plate) in corresponding groups. There was no significant (P > 0.05) differences observed in the quantities of 11-cis ROL (D, right), all-trans ROL (E, right), or all-trans RAL (F, right) between dark-reared Rpe65-transfected and control cells. Bleaching Rpe65-transfected 661W cells with LWL light resulted in a significant (P < 0.01) decrease in the quantity of 11-cis RE (1.06 ± 0.19 nmol/plate) (B, left) that corresponded to a significant (P < 0.001) increase in the quantity of all-trans RAL (0.98 ± 0.11 nmol/plate) compared to control cells (11-cis RE: 1.89 ± 0.21 nmol/plate; all-trans RAL: 0.09 ± 0.02 nmol/plate) (F, left). No significant (P > 0.05) differences were observed in the quantities of all-trans ROL (E, left) or 11-cis ROL (D, left) between the two groups of cells after LWL-light bleaching; however, the quantities of 11-cis ROL in both groups of cells were significantly (P < 0.01) reduced compared to corresponding groups that were dark-reared (D, right). n = 8 for each condition. Error bars represent s.d. * indicates significance.

caption.title: 11-cis RAL synthesis is detected in RPE65/rhodopsin-transfected 661W cells
caption.p: RPE65/rhodopsin-transfected 661W cells were incubated with 10 μM of 11-cis ROL, all-trans ROL, or vehicle for 24 hours in dark conditions. Representative HPLC chromatograph of extracted retinoids from cells incubated with 11-cis ROL (A). The indicated portion of the chromatograph is expanded in B and displayed for cells incubated with 11-cis ROL (blue), all-trans ROL (red) or vehicle (black). Presence of 11-cis RAL is only detected in cells incubated with 11-cis ROL.

caption.title: Specificities of anti-RPE65 antibodies
caption.p: Two polyclonal anti-RPE65 antibodies targeting distant epitopes of RPE65 protein are used for immunohistochemistry analysis. B = bovine; M = mouse; H = human.
